content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 21, 1990


Anthony L. Miller, Chief Deputy
Office of the Secretary of State
1230 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-049

Dear Mr. Miller:

You have requested informal assistance with regard to any
reporting or other responsibilities arising under the Political
Reform Act ("Act")¹ as a result of the Secretary of State's
involvement in the California Voter Foundation (the "Foundation").

QUESTION

1. Does the Secretary of State's involvement in the Foundation, as incorporator, give rise to any reporting or other responsibilities under the Act?

2. If so, would such responsibilities be eliminated by removing the Secretary of State from involvement in the administration of the Foundation?

CONCLUSION

1 and 2. The mere fact that the Secretary of State is an incorporator of the Foundation does not cause the Foundation to incur any reporting or other responsibilities under the Act.

________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-049
Page 2


FACTS

The California Voter Foundation is a nonprofit corporation
which has qualified for tax-exempt status under Section 501(c)(3)
of the Internal Revenue Code. It was organized last year to
receive private donations to be used to supplement voter registra-
tion outreach and educational efforts traditionally conducted by
the Office of the Secretary of State. These efforts include bus
signs, billboards, bill stuffers, public service announcements, a
toll-free registration form request number, placards, contests,
private-sector registration drives and so forth. The drives are
nonpartisan.

The Secretary of State was listed as the incorporator of the
Foundation, but she has had no direct personal involvement in
either creating or organizing the Foundation. You and two former
staff members of the Secretary of State's office were listed as
the initial officers of the Foundation. The only money received
by the Foundation has come from participants in a voting seminar
held last year. No substantial money has been spent to date.

ANALYSIS

The Foundation would become subject to the Act if it
qualified as a committee (Section 82013) or a controlled committee
(Section 82016.) Those sections provide as follows:

"Committee" means any person or combination of
persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year;

(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year; or

(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or committees.

A person or combination of persons that
becomes a committee shall retain its status as a
committee until such time as that status is
terminated pursuant to Section 84214.

Section 82013.

"Controlled committee" means a committee which
is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate

Our File No. I-90-049
Page 3

or state measure proponent or which acts jointly
with a candidate, controlled committee or state
measure proponent in connection with the making of
expenditures. A candidate or state measure
proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or
any other committee he controls has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the com-
mittee.

Section 82016.

Under the facts which you have presented, the Foundation does
not qualify as a committee or as a controlled committee. A
nonprofit corporation which engages in nonpartisan voter registra-
tion is not receiving or making contributions. (In re Welsh,
4 FPPC Ops. 78 (1978), copy enclosed.)

The mere involvement of an elected officer in the incorpora-
tion of a nonprofit organization does not mean that the organiza-
tion will be treated as a committee or controlled committee under
the Act. The organization would have to be receiving contribu-
tions to be used for political purposes or making contributions to
candidates or measures.

You indicate that present and former members of the Secretary
of State's staff are listed as initial officers of the Foundation.
If the officers of the Foundation act at the behest of the
Secretary of State to control the expenditures of the Foundation,
they would be considered agents of the Secretary of State. If the
Foundation's funds are used to produce materials or advertisements
which prominently feature the Secretary of State during an elec-
tion campaign, it is possible that a contribution could occur.
(Baird Advice Letter, No. A-82-096, copy enclosed.) This could
result in the Foundation being treated as a controlled committee.
However, under the facts presented, the Foundation will not incur
any reporting or other responsibilities under the Act.

The Commission is considering adoption of a proposed regula-
tion on the subject of whether a nonprofit corporation organized
under Section 501(c)(3) may become a controlled committee.2 The
proposed regulation is currently scheduled for hearing at the
March 13 meeting of the Commission.

2 A copy of proposed Regulation 18217 is enclosed for your
review.

Our File No. I-90-049
Page 4

I trust this answers the questions that you have posed. If
you have any questions or require any further information, please
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Margaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division

KED/MWE/aa

Enclosures
